questions.question: What are the three legs of the Pediatric Assessment Triangle?
options: A. Airway, Breathing, Circulation
B. Appearance, Work of Breathing, Circulation to Skin
C. History, Exam, Vital Signs
D. Tone, Color, Cry
questions.rationale: The PAT's three legs are Appearance, Work of Breathing, and Circulation to Skin — assessed together from across the room before any hands-on contact.

questions.question: What does the TICLS mnemonic help assess?
options: A. Work of Breathing
B. Circulation to Skin
C. Appearance
D. Vital signs
questions.rationale: TICLS (Tone, Interactiveness, Consolability, Look/Gaze, Speech/Cry) breaks down the Appearance leg of the PAT into observable components.

questions.question: A child is limp, will not make eye contact, and is inconsolable, but breathing appears effortless and skin color is normal. What does this pattern suggest?
options: A. A respiratory problem
B. A primarily CNS/metabolic problem
C. A shock/circulatory problem
D. Cardiopulmonary failure
questions.rationale: Abnormal Appearance alone, with normal Work of Breathing and Circulation, points toward a CNS/metabolic category rather than a respiratory or circulatory problem.

questions.question: A child has abnormal Appearance (lethargic) AND visible retractions with nasal flaring, but normal skin color. What is the likely category?
options: A. CNS/metabolic problem
B. Respiratory problem
C. Shock
D. Cardiopulmonary failure
questions.rationale: Abnormal Appearance combined with abnormal Work of Breathing (retractions, flaring), with normal circulation, points to a respiratory category.

questions.question: A child is lethargic and has mottled, cool skin, but breathing appears unlabored with no retractions. What is the likely category?
options: A. Respiratory problem
B. Shock/circulatory problem
C. Normal finding
D. Isolated CNS problem only
questions.rationale: Abnormal Appearance plus abnormal Circulation to Skin, with normal Work of Breathing, suggests a shock/circulatory category.

questions.question: A child shows abnormal findings across ALL three PAT legs. What does this represent?
options: A. A mild, self-limited illness
B. Cardiopulmonary failure — the highest-urgency category
C. Normal variation
D. An isolated skin condition
questions.rationale: Abnormality across all three legs indicates a severe, multi-system problem — cardiopulmonary failure — requiring the most urgent response.

questions.question: Is the PAT performed with a stethoscope or blood pressure cuff?
options: A. Yes, both are required
B. No — it is entirely hands-off, visual and auditory, from across the room
C. Only a stethoscope is used
D. Only a BP cuff is used
questions.rationale: The PAT is deliberately hands-off, performed by observation and listening from a distance before any equipment or touching begins.

questions.question: A child looks well on the PAT. What should the provider do next?
options: A. Skip the primary assessment since the PAT was reassuring
B. Proceed to a full hands-on primary assessment at a calmer pace
C. Discharge the patient immediately
D. Only check vital signs, skip any exam
questions.rationale: The PAT never replaces the hands-on primary assessment — a reassuring PAT sets a calmer pace, but the full ABCDE assessment still follows.

questions.question: Why is Work of Breathing considered an 'early window' into respiratory status?
options: A. It only becomes abnormal after the child has already desaturated
B. Increased effort reflects compensation and can appear before oxygen saturation falls
C. It has no relationship to oxygenation
D. It is only useful in adults
questions.rationale: Increased work of breathing is a compensatory response that often precedes a measurable drop in SpO2, making it an early indicator of respiratory compromise.

questions.question: Which finding is part of the Circulation to Skin leg of the PAT?
options: A. Retractions
B. Nasal flaring
C. Mottling
D. Stridor
questions.rationale: Mottling is a skin-color finding assessed under Circulation to Skin; retractions, flaring, and stridor belong to the Work of Breathing leg.

questions.question: Why is Appearance considered the most sensitive PAT leg?
options: A. It is the easiest to measure with equipment
B. It reflects the adequacy of oxygenation, ventilation, brain perfusion, and CNS function all at once
C. It is only relevant in trauma
D. It replaces the need for the other two legs
questions.rationale: Appearance integrates multiple physiologic systems, making it a highly sensitive early indicator — an abnormal Appearance is a red flag even if the other two legs look normal.

questions.question: What is meant by assessing the PAT 'from the doorway'?
options: A. Only assessing patients who are near an actual doorway
B. The PAT is completed within the first seconds of encountering the child, before approaching or touching them
C. The PAT requires the child to be standing in a doorway
D. This phrase has no specific meaning in PEPP
questions.rationale: The 'from the doorway' framing captures how quickly and unobtrusively the PAT is performed — a rapid visual/auditory read taken immediately upon encountering the child.

questions.question: A muffled, weak cry in an infant is a finding under which PAT leg?
options: A. Work of Breathing
B. Circulation to Skin
C. Appearance (Speech/Cry component of TICLS)
D. None of the legs
questions.rationale: Speech/Cry is one of the five TICLS components under the Appearance leg; a weak or muffled cry is an abnormal Appearance finding.

questions.question: Why does the PAT drive 'the pace of everything that follows' rather than the content of care?
options: A. It determines drug doses directly
B. It sets the general impression and urgency level, shaping how quickly the primary assessment proceeds and whether interventions happen simultaneously
C. It has no influence on subsequent care
D. It replaces vital sign measurement entirely
questions.rationale: The PAT's purpose is to establish urgency and likely category quickly, which shapes the speed and sequencing of the ensuing primary assessment and interventions — not to dictate specific treatments by itself.

questions.question: Which combination of PAT findings would most strongly suggest the HIGHEST acuity requiring the most urgent response?
options: A. Normal Appearance, normal Work of Breathing, normal Circulation
B. Abnormal Appearance, Work of Breathing, AND Circulation to Skin all together
C. Mild nasal flaring only, otherwise normal
D. Slightly pale skin with normal Appearance and breathing
questions.rationale: Abnormalities across all three PAT legs together represent the cardiopulmonary failure category, the highest-urgency PAT pattern.

questions.question: How should skin color findings under Circulation to Skin be interpreted?
options: A. Against a universal standard regardless of the individual child
B. Against the child's own baseline skin tone, in adequate lighting
C. Only cyanosis matters; pallor and mottling are irrelevant
D. Skin color cannot be reliably assessed in the field
questions.rationale: Skin findings are compared to the individual child's baseline tone under good lighting, since a fixed universal standard would not account for normal variation between children.
